authors: Rahul Seetharaman, Aman Bansal, Hamed Zamani, Kaustubh D. Dhole

breakthroughAssessment.justification: The paper presents the first systematic study of scaling laws specifically for rerankers, a crucial but underexplored component of multi-stage information retrieval systems. It demonstrates that reranker performance, particularly NDCG@10, follows predictable power laws with model size and data, enabling accurate forecasting of large-model performance from smaller-scale training runs. This offers significant practical value by saving computational resources in training expensive large-scale rerankers. The work also highlights how scaling behavior differs across pointwise, pairwise, and listwise objectives, providing actionable insights for practitioners.

coreProblem.concreteExample: The high cost of training large-scale reranking models makes it crucial to accurately forecast the performance of a 1B-parameter model using only smaller-scale training runs (e.g., up to 400M parameters) to save computational resources.
coreProblem.statement: While scaling laws are well-established for natural language generation and dense retrieval, they are largely underexplored for reranking systems. Reranking presents unique challenges due to operating on retriever-induced candidate sets, heterogeneous learning-to-rank objectives, and discontinuous top-k metrics like NDCG, making scaling behavior non-obvious.
whyItMatters: Enables accurate forecasting of large reranker performance from smaller-scale training runs.
Provides a robust methodology for saving significant computational resources during model development.
Facilitates planning expensive training at 1B+ scale with fewer full-size experiments.
Offers actionable insights for building industrial-grade retrieval systems.

evaluationHighlights: Reranker performance follows predictable power laws with model size and data scale for metrics like NDCG@10, MAP, and MRR.
Accurate forecasting of large-model reranker performance (e.g., NDCG@10 for 1B-parameter models) is possible using only smaller-scale training runs.
Scaling behavior (fit quality and apparent exponents) differs across pointwise, pairwise, and listwise fine-tuning objectives.
Contrastive Entropy (CE) is less reliable for reranker forecasting compared to NDCG@10 due to sensitivity to score calibration and normalization.

evaluationSetup.setting: Cross-encoder rerankers (Ettin series) finetuned on 100k MSMARCO queries, with BM25 as the first-stage retriever. Evaluation includes in-domain (MSMARCO-dev) and out-of-domain (TREC DL, DL HARD) datasets. Power-law functions are fitted and predictive accuracy evaluated via held-out checkpoints using RMSE.

experimentFigures.caption: Scaling behavior of NDCG@10 (top row) and Contrastive Entropy (bottom row) under model, data, and joint scaling.
Model scaling trends on TREC DL ’19 across NDCG@10, MAP, and MRR.

keyResultGroups.title: TREC DL Forecasting Errors (NDCG, MAP, MRR)

mainTakeaways: Reranker performance follows predictable power laws with model and data scale for traditional metrics like NDCG@10.
Accurate forecasting of downstream ranking quality (e.g., NDCG@10) in the large-model regime is possible using only smaller-scale training runs.
This provides a practical approach for planning expensive reranker training by reducing the need for full-scale experiments.

Scaling behavior (fit quality and apparent exponents) differs across pointwise, pairwise, and listwise objectives, indicating that a single universal fit is unlikely to capture all paradigms equally well.

explanation: Presents the first systematic study of scaling laws for rerankers across pointwise, pairwise, and listwise learning-to-rank paradigms.
Demonstrates that reranker performance (e.g., NDCG@10) follows predictable power laws with model size, training data exposure, and their joint effect.
Shows that the performance of a 1B-parameter reranker can be accurately forecast using smaller-scale training runs (up to 400M parameters) across various metrics and datasets.
Highlights that scaling behavior and forecasting errors differ significantly across the three reranking objectives.
keyNovelty.main: First systematic study characterizing scaling laws for rerankers in information retrieval, enabling accurate performance forecasting for large models from smaller-scale experiments across various learning-to-rank objectives.

keyTerms.definition: A final and most influential step in multi-stage retrieval pipelines that reorders a relatively small set of candidates retrieved by an earlier stage to maximize ranking quality.
Empirical regularities where model performance follows predictable patterns (e.g., power laws) as computational resources (model size, data, compute) increase.
A widely used top-k ranking metric that measures the usefulness or gain of a document based on its position in the ranked list, with a logarithmic reduction for lower ranks.
A continuous diagnostic metric used to analyze scaling, representing the negative log-probability of ranking a relevant passage above negative passages, similar to proxy losses like perplexity.

comparisonToPriorWork: Prior work on scaling laws has focused on natural language generation (e.g., language model loss) and dense retrieval (e.g., first-stage retrieval quality), which are not directly applicable to reranking due to its unique characteristics.
Unlike dense retrieval, rerankers operate on a candidate set induced by a first-stage retriever (e.g., BM25), making the decision space conditional on upstream retrieval.
Existing scaling analyses for retrieval components often focus on approximate candidate selection over a corpus rather than fine-grained reranking over a retriever-induced candidate set.

The paper extends the understanding of scaling behavior to downstream ranking quality metrics (e.g., NDCG@10) rather than solely perplexity-style objectives or continuous proxy losses, which have shown limitations in direct forecasting for retrieval.
limitations: Contrastive Entropy (CE) is less reliable for reranker forecasting compared to NDCG@10, as it is sensitive to score calibration and normalization, which can cause fluctuations in trends.
Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR) does not show predictable scaling on all evaluation datasets, particularly on TREC DL '19.

A single universal power-law fit is unlikely to capture the scaling behavior equally well across all pointwise, pairwise, and listwise paradigms due to observed differences in fit quality and exponents.

modeling.label: Model Size Scaling
modeling.value: Modeled using a saturating power law: $M(M) = a - b M^{-c}$, where $M$ is the number of parameters, $a$ is the asymptotic performance ceiling, $b$ controls scale of gains, and $c$ is the model-scaling exponent.

modeling.label: Data Size Scaling
modeling.value: Modeled using an analogous power law: $M(S) = a - b S^{-c}$, where $S$ is the training step or consumed examples, $a$ is the plateau NDCG, and $c$ captures how quickly performance saturates.

modeling.label: Joint Scaling (Model and Data)
modeling.value: Modeled using an additive joint scaling law: $M(M, S) = a - b M^{-\alpha} - c S^{-\beta}$, where $\alpha$ and $\beta$ quantify respective returns to scaling model size and training exposure, and $a$ is the joint asymptote.

modeling.label: Pointwise Reranking Objective
modeling.value: The model predicts a per-document relevance label $y$ for a tuple $(q, d, y)$ by minimizing Binary Cross Entropy Loss over individual scores $f_{\theta}(q, d_i)$.

modeling.label: Pairwise Reranking Objective
modeling.value: The model is trained on triplets $(q, d^+, d^-)$ such that $f_{\theta}(q, d^+) > f_{\theta}(q, d^-)$ via a pairwise margin loss, specifically RankNet loss.

modeling.label: Listwise Reranking Objective
modeling.value: The model is trained on a query with a full document list $(q, \mathbf{d}, \mathbf{y})$ using a permutation-aware, list-level objective, specifically ListNet loss.

pipelineFlow: Train a family of cross-encoder reranking models (Ettin series) across increasing model sizes (17M to 400M) and increasing data exposure (up to 100k queries).
Evaluate trained models using both a proxy metric (Contrastive Entropy) and a downstream metric (NDCG@10, MAP, MRR).
Fit appropriate power-law functions (model-size, data-size, and joint scaling) to the observed performance trends.
Validate the predictive power by holding out the last N points of the scaling curve (e.g., 1B model performance from 400M models) and calculating RMSE/MAE for the extrapolated estimates.

problemDefinition.label: Reranker Scoring Function
problemDefinition.value: Given a query $q$ and candidate set $C(q) = \{d_1, \ldots, d_K\}$, a reranker computes a scoring function $f_{\theta}(q, d_i) \mapsto s_i \in \mathbb{R}$ that induces a ranking $\pi(q) = \text{argsort}_i s_i$.

problemDefinition.label: NDCG@k Metric
problemDefinition.value: $\text{NDCG}@k = \frac{\sum_{r=1}^k 2^{\text{rel}_r}-1 / \log_2(r+1)}{\sum_{r=1}^k 2^{\text{rel}_r^*}-1 / \log_2(r+1)}$, where $\text{rel}_r$ is relevance grade at position $r$ by the model and $\text{rel}_r^*$ is the ideal relevance grade at position $r$.

problemDefinition.label: Contrastive Entropy Metric
problemDefinition.value: $CE = -\log \frac{\exp(s(q_i, p_i^+; \theta))}{\exp(s(q_i, p_i^+; \theta)) + \sum_j \exp(s(q_i, p_j^-; \theta))}$, where $q_i$ is the $i$-th query, $p_i^+$ is a relevant passage, $p_j^-$ are sampled non-relevant passages, and $s(q, p; \theta)$ is the model scoring function.

technicalDetails.reproducibility: The authors state that the code and evaluation protocols used for training, evaluation, and large-scale performance forecasting will be released.

thesis: Reranker performance follows predictable power laws with model and data scale, allowing accurate forecasting of large-model performance from smaller-scale training runs and providing a practical approach for efficient planning of expensive reranker training.
title: Scaling Laws for Reranking in Information Retrieval
